cta.contactSubtitle: Game jam idea, junior role, or just coffee — I reply quickly.

about.title: About Me
about.subtitle: PLAYER PROFILE
paragraphs: Hey, I'm Dennis. Gameplay Programmer with a Master's in Game Development.

I build systems that feel good to play. Twin-stick combat, tower defense, AR multiplayer, ML-driven Pong paddles — if it's playable, I want to know how it ticks. Six years with Unity, plus Unreal, FUDGE and C++.

One of my favorite projects: a haptic VR room without controllers. Instead of joysticks, real-world objects are mapped one-to-one to their virtual counterparts. Grab a sword in reality and you grab it in VR too. Genuinely feels like magic.

What I otherwise love: game jams. 48 hours, one idea, a team, and at the end something actually runs. No planning committee, no ticket system, just code and coffee. I learned more about gamedev there than in any course.

descriptions.unthroned:
Twin-stick shooter with base-building and tower-defense elements, built over two semesters as our Master's project. You play Dave, a dethroned necromancer reclaiming his brother's empire.

The core system: a day/night cycle where you summon souls during the day to gather resources for you. Sounds like a no-brainer — summon as many as possible. But at night those same souls turn on you, and you fight every single one back. Players set their own difficulty: more souls = more resources = harder nights. Originally summoning was unlimited, but performance forced us to cap it.

Team of three — I was the only dev, plus two 2D artists. My focus was core combat, enemy AI, the summon and upgrade systems — and way too much time in a custom sprite-sorting algorithm. Isometric 2D with lots of movement and angled objects like portals turns render order into an art form. You can still spot the occasional glitch in the trailer, but the bulk of it sits. We also rewrote the controls many times — twin-stick as a genre was settled early, but the feel (aim direction, rotation while shooting, button mapping) only clicked after heavy playtesting.

We also had an external composer on board: a friend from earlier projects who submitted our soundtrack as his own two-semester project at his uni — multiple custom tracks written for us.
descriptions.oasis:
I built Oasis over two semesters. The first was a study project where the game itself came together: an escape room in Unreal with three levels and one puzzle each, still classic controller-based. In the second semester it became my bachelor's thesis, and the real centerpiece arrived — the haptic VR room, the physical installation behind the game.

We'd originally planned the whole thing with a motion-capture suit and gloves. Then the chip shortage hit and the ordered system just never arrived in time. So I pivoted and hacked everything together with HTC Vive Trackers instead — which ended up working surprisingly well.

The core idea: instead of controllers, the player picks up real objects. The university provided a physical room and access to a 3D printer. Puzzle props hung on the walls via magnets, each connected to a tracker that synced its position into VR. To make the virtual world match the real geometry precisely, I scanned the entire room in 3D with my iPad's LIDAR. Those scans let me place every prop in Unreal down to the millimeter — at the start of each session the player just had to tap three calibration points and the whole room snapped into place.

Each level had a weapon — sword, staff, book — and a matching puzzle. With the sword you redirected lasers to arrange floating islands in the right order. With the book you slotted orbs into matching sockets. With the staff you were supposed to guide butterflies, but that last puzzle I didn't finish in time.

The cleverest part of the system were the magnets. In VR you're holding a full sword; physically it's just a grip with a sensor. When you bring the weapon back to its designated spot on the wall, the magnet pulls it into place — feels like the VR world is taking the object out of your hand. The orbs were plastic spheres with sensors inside; their wall-mounted sockets were half-round magnet cups, so the spheres appear to float in midair.

Level design also had to disguise the physical room's limits. My solution: everything happens on floating islands in the sky. Nobody willingly steps into open air, which meant I could place islands only where I wanted the player to go. By cleverly rotating the same floor plan, I could also hide the fact that you stay in one single room the whole time, and that the sword grip and staff grip are physically the same prop.

And finally the little tutorial ghost. A balloon with a fabric cover, mounted to the ceiling, controlled via a motor and a Raspberry Pi. The game told the Pi over a server call when to lower the ghost and when to retract it. During the tutorial the player could actually touch it; once the tutorial was done, it was simply gone — the project where I learned the most about hardware-software integration.
descriptions.aurea:
A low-poly AR multiplayer game for smartphones. Unity AR Foundation turns your living room into an arena — models in Blender and Maya, netcode for small matches, playful vibe.

My focus: AR plane detection, multiplayer sync via a relay server, and the gameplay feel.
descriptions.cozyWinter:
A cozy Christmas-market builder. No fail state, no deadline — just you, a blanket and some mulled wine. Place buildings, add decorations, watch the customers roll in.

Built during a game jam as an antidote to all stress games. I did nearly everything: code, design, balance.
descriptions.pumpkinHarvest:
A relaxed farming game. Plant, water, harvest, deliver — or just take a walk in the woods. The suburb wants pumpkins; you deliver. Atmosphere-first, no shooter-adrenaline curve.

Game-jam submission, built solo.
descriptions.witchWay:
Indirect control: you don't steer the witch, you shape the forest around her. Place objects, lay down arrows, keep her alive as long as you can. Every choice has side effects.

Student project in a game-jam format, team of three.
descriptions.gravityPong:
Pong — with gravity, and an ML agent as opponent. Trained with Unity ML-Agents, learns to steer the paddle itself. A small experiment where debugging the training was the real game.

Hobby project, fully solo.
descriptions.bomberman:
A Bomberman clone, built in the FUDGE engine at HFU. No prebuilt framework, every gameplay system from scratch.

Student project for the course "Development of Interactive Applications", team work focused on grid logic and bomb simulation.
descriptions.arcReactor:
3D model — Tony Stark's Arc Reactor, high-poly with PBR textures. Modeled in Blender, textured in Substance Painter.

Student project on game-asset creation.
descriptions.mjolnir:
3D model — Thor's hammer, high-poly, PBR textures with scratches and wear. Blender plus Substance Painter.

Student project on game-asset creation.
descriptions.coffeeExe:
Top-secret long-running project: reliably convert coffee into runnable code. Shipping since 1996, crashes without espresso, performance scales proportionally to bean strength.

Only known balance issue: excessive sugar introduces unexpected features. Pull requests (in bean form) welcome.
